records.ety: [OE. rusche, rische, resche, AS. risce, akin to LG. rusk, risch, D. & G. rusch; all probably fr. L. ruscum butcher's broom; akin to Goth. raus reed, G. rohr.]

defs: A name given to many aquatic or marsh-growing endogenous plants with soft, slender stems, as the species of Juncus and Scirpus.
notes: ☞ Some species are used in bottoming chairs and plaiting mats, and the pith is used in some places for wicks to lamps and rushlights.

quotes: John Bull's friendship is not worth a rush.

records.ety: [OE. ruschen; cf. AS. hryscan to make a noise, D. ruischen to rustle, G. rauschen, MHG. rūschen to rush, to rustle, LG. rusken, OSw. ruska, Icel. & Sw. ruska to shake, Dan. ruske to shake, and E. rouse.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Rushed (rsht); p. pr. & vb. n. Rushing.]

defs: To move forward with impetuosity, violence, and tumultuous rapidity or haste; as, armies rush to battle; waters rush down a precipice.
quotes: Like to an entered tide, they all rush by.

defs: To enter into something with undue haste and eagerness, or without due deliberation and preparation; as, to rush business or speculation.
quotes: They . . . never think it to be a part of religion to rush into the office of princes and ministers.

defs: To push or urge forward with impetuosity or violence; to hurry forward.
To recite (a lesson) or pass (an examination) without an error.
A moving forward with rapidity and force or eagerness; a violent motion or course; as, a rush of troops; a rush of winds; a rush of water.
quotes: A gentleman of his train spurred up his horse, and, with a violent rush, severed him from the duke.

defs: Great activity with pressure; as, a rush of business.
A rusher; as, the center rush, whose place is in the center of the rush line; the end rush.
The act of running with the ball.
